speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is July 25, 1995. So Mila, you were telling me that the British just liberated Bergen-Belsen. And where were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was April 15, 1945 when the British army came into Bergen-Belsen. I was in the barracks. I was-- my fever had-- my fever had broken, but the dysentery and the typhus and the headaches and the hair loss and everything in me was dead except my heart was going. And when they came into the barrack, and they saw me laying there, they had a stretcher with them and-- to take me to a hospital, of course. And I said, I remember motioning, 'No hospital, no hospital.' And they said to me, then you cannot live.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said something, but I didn't speak English. 'Hospital,' 'szpital' in Polish, 'hospital' in Jewish-- I knew what 'hospital' is. You know? And I said again, 'No hospital.' But I said to him-- I-- I showed him the lice. I said--

speaker: interviewee
text: [BLOWING] They brought in those things where they were disinfecting, you know, the-- the-- the spritzers, and they knew what I meant. They brought this in with a certain chemical, and they sprayed it on my body. And you know,m it was like those lice that were embedded in me for such a long-- they looked like spiders already. That's how big they were, drinking the blood for so many months. And they started to fall off, you know, and I started to feel so light. You know, like-- but you know, my body was all infected. I was all pus. And this took a long time for it to heal. So what they did is they brought in a [? shisle, ?] you know, like a-- like a-- a pan, a big pan, filled it with water, and they washed me with a chemical very carefully. With a disinfectant, possibly, and they washed me very gently to wash away the dirt, and the pus was flowing. And then they used a salve, a certain ointment, and they smeared me up from head to toe. And they took me out from this part of the barrack, and they put me into a different barrack. I still did not want to go to the hospital. Then, they were transferred from Bergen-Belsen to Bergen, where the Nazis had their headquarters. We took their barracks. See, Bergen-Belsen was divided, Bergen dash Belsen. So we had the camp in Belsen, and this was Bergen. And-- but prior to this, they put me on the scale to weigh me after I was cleaned up. And they had a special-- one of those special scales where they carried-- they had in their ambulances, whatever-- you know, the medics. And they put me on that scale, and this guy is holding me, you know. He's trying to hold me, not to put any pressure on. And I hear this guy say to him, 50 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 pounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 pounds. And why do I understand 50 pounds? Again, funfzig pfund, 50 pounds. It's very-- every language, if you hear one or two words, you can deduct what the meaning. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- I didn't feel like I weigh anything. I felt like I'm dead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I looked skeletal. You know? I-- I-- I don't know if I looked-- if I looked at all. I had no hair. I had no-- I looked like something-- you know. What happened to your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost it from the typhus. And the doctor said my hair will never grow back. OK? Shows you how wrong they were. And what happened is they took us to-- they took us to-- to Lingen. I went in to a German woman, because the Jewish people wouldn't help me with food. You Know?' Everybody was for themselves. I was laying there in this other place, and I said, if the Germans didn't kill me, I'll die now. I said, I don't want to die now. [CHUCKLES] So I picked myself up, and I went into the first German little gate I saw. And this German, Frau [NON-ENGLISH], was very nice to me. She said her son-in-law is an SS man. She tells me her son-in-law is an SS man, your-- your-- oh-- zo-- zo-- something, I forgot what you call it in German, son-in-law. And she puts me in the garden, and her daughter is there, and with her little granddaughter-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Marissa. Marissa. And she would give me the-- the grapes into my mouth, and she would feed me the pig meat. And every day, he would come and measure my hand, Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. And my neck-- I was like a-- they made-- they made me a skirt, like the tall, you know, the [INAUDIBLE] in Austria. And then, they Jewish girls, the prisoners, broke into her. She saw that they're attacking the house, and she had rabbits, and she had pigs. They would slaughtering them, you know. So she says to me, Emilia-- she gave me a name, Emilia-- so Emilia-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Tell the girls they should leave my house alone. Tell them I'm good to you, and they will leave me alone. I said, Frau [NON-ENGLISH], I said, if you took me in to be a shield for your house, I said, sorry, I cannot do that, because whatever you-- those kids are, it is you who made it. You created these monsters, I said, and you have to suffer now. I said, maybe your time has come. I picked myself up, and I left the place. I wanted no part of it. I went back, and I was in-- in Bergen. And then, I had a friend, Rose-- Rose and Sala Rosenbaum. They are in Australia. We were like sisters because they lived in Chebinyat, and this was their home. And Rusia said-- after the war had ended-- she says, you know, Mila, I am going out of this camp, and I'm going to see if I can find anybody alive from my family. She had three brothers, Roma, Gersig, and Bennig. I said, Rusia, if you are leaving, I said, do me a favor. Sala will be with me. Her younger sister was my age. She was the older one. I said, and you see if you can find maybe Moniek Zagorski. But I knew that he maybe should be alive because he was in-- he left in good shape, you know, and I-- I had a feeling. My other brother was dead. My little brother was dead. I knew that. But he was my-- I had it in my mind that this child must be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, good, I will do that. She kissed me and Sala, and she left Bergen. And it was-- I don't know-- a month later, maybe six weeks? She-- somebody knocks at my door in Bergen-- in Bergen. And I go to the door, and I open the door. And who is standing in front? It's my brother, Moniek. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What can I tell you? I didn't see him four years-- 1941. I went in 1942. Three and a half years, I was there-- four years, a little over. He looked like-- he looked like a very sick Paul Newman. A sick Paul Neuman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- just like the picture in the-- in the den-- I'll show it to you-- when he was liberated. I don't know how long we stood, just locked in each other's-- in each other's arms. We just were locked and cried and cried and cried. You see, he didn't see anything that happened at home because he left very early. So when I tell him the story, we sit, and we cry. And he comes to me for Shabbat many times. For Friday nights, I cook his Shabbat dinner. We sing [? smiros ?] with them, like we did at home and we both cried because it was so beautiful. And then, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anyway, he took-- he smuggled me and Sala, Sally, my-- my-- the sister from Rusia. He got passports for us. I don't know how he arranged that, that he has-- he found two sisters. And he smuggled us, and they kept on a train, on a coal train, under the coals, because the border from Germany to Austria was still closed. And any smuggling of people could mean jail or-- or death, if possible. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had us under the coal on this train, and he had the-- the thing anyway, in case they find us. But it passed. We came to Germany-- he-- to Austria, and he-- and we got off the train. And he took us to-- to the [? Foyekogel ?] in [NON-ENGLISH], which was a resort area. You must have heard of where Hitler was born, where Hitler had his thing in Austria, his bunker. I forgot. I-- I forgot. It's good I forgot. And I was in this convalescent. Took me a long time to let this German doctor touch me, Dr. Mueller, because I always thought that they will kill me. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in 19-- in 1945, I-- I was-- from 1945 to 1947, I was in Austria, where I recuperated. My hair started to grow back. I got my strength a little bit back, and I stayed already in the apartment, but the hospital was a little bit away. And then, my brother said, Mila, I think I remember the address of uncle. We have an uncle in America, my father's youngest brother. He left at the age of 13 from Poland. He said, I think we'll contact him. And my cousin Charlie, who also survived together with my brother, he was also in Austria. They both remembered the correct address, so they wrote to him, and they got in touch, and we were sent affidavits. On March 3, 1947, we arrived in the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were about three days circling the Statue of Liberty. And I don't think-- I had such-- genuine happiness, I would not say, because genuine happiness was never in me anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I cannot every say that I am genuinely happy. I was glad to be alive. I was glad  I found somebody in my family. At least, we have each other, like 'Hansel and Gretel' sort of thing. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, we disembarked on the third of March. No, about two days later, so the fifth. We got off the boat, and uncle was waiting. I recognized him immediately because he looked like my dad very much. And I came to Northvale, New Jersey, where I stayed with him for about five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and I and my cousins. One cousin came a year before I did to this country. And then, in 1953, I went to a-- in 1952, I went to a party, and I was introduced to this gentleman of mine, and we got married. We will be married-- we are married 42 years, Max and I. We have beautiful children. I am-- my life is OK. I don't want to say I'm-- I'm-- I-- I cannot say I'm genuinely happy. I'm happy. I have my family, I have my husband, I have my home, but I wish-- I wish all this did not happen. Not this. I wish this-- this whole thing would not have happened, this Holocaust. It would have been a beautiful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you kept your faith in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: I keep my faith in God. I believe in him very strongly. My children attended yeshiva. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like I said before, this was before [? food. ?] I kept it going that way, and I'm very proud of myself that I accomplished it. My children are wonderful human beings, beautiful, decent children. And for this, I'm thankful. I don't want any credit for that. I thank God that he gave them the wisdom so they'd follow the right path in their life, and may their children continue to do so, too. They all go to yeshiva, my grandchildren, each and every one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you believe strongly in speaking about the Holocaust and Holocaust education.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is my focal point in my life now, is the Holocaust. Because I feel that the world must be informed. The world must be told, and they must be told by survivors, because we are the last people. After us, there'll be no one left. And it's being denied while we are here. Can you imagine what will happen after we are gone? I believe strongly in love. I feel that love is a gift that God gave us, and it's something that nobody can take from you. It will follow you from generation to generation. And this seed of love must be spread around the world. We must nourish the world with it if we want to-- if want to have peace on Earth. With hate, we accomplish nothing. Hate is a-- is a disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me earlier that you have gone to the schools and given lectures to the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak for the last nine, 10 years in schools. I have excellent results from my speeches. Children are very receptive. Children do want to know. I am telling everybody, we must speak to them. Children want to know. They're very inquisitive. They want to learn. And they tell me that only through education-- I tell it to them, not they tell it to me-- I told them that only through education can we accomplish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we want to accomplish, love and education. I tell them this, and they know it. You can imagine-- you can imagine 6 million innocent souls destined to be born will never walk this Earth again. And the contribution that they may have made to this world will never be realized. We cannot allow this to happen again. So we must do what we must do at no cost. There should be no doubt in anybody's mind that by speaking, it does not help. It helps a great deal. I have some letters. I have some itinerary from children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You will-- you will understand when I read one or two letters what it means to me and how much they want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to read--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love to read, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one of the letters from Duncan Middle School. It was written by Jessica Fleischer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 'Dear Mila, words cannot describe the feelings and the emotions we experienced Monday. You have touched a part of us we never knew was there. You are a strong and intelligent woman, a definite role model for us all. You have opened up our hearts and have made us realize we have to teach our generation what happened and how cruel men can be to men. You have shared hard and disturbing times with us. For that, we feel that no one will ever forget you. We know that we won't. You are extremely special and will always remain in our hearts. We just wish that you could have stayed longer in class with us. We love you. Love, enclosed is our addresses, three children.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this is an essay written by a young boy of 13. When I spoke in Duncan Middle School in 1993-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1992, this is, pardon me. And this young boy had insulted a black teacher. He-- he was insolent to her. He said that he hates the blacks, and the Jews even more so. And my teacher told me, Mila, this boy is going to be in class. He knows a Holocaust survivor's coming to speak. He may walk out. He may not show up. And I told her, Loretta, whatever this young man will want to do, he may do. I will continue what I must do. And she said to me, I'm going to-- I'm going to point to you when he's there, so you know who he is. And sure enough, I came in, and he was sitting in his chair, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very hateful. You could see the hate in his eyes, you know, but very good-looking. And I started talking. I was very-- I was very direct, very to the point, and I let them know what men do to men and what the-- the hate groups do accomplish. They accomplish nothing but hate, destruction, and death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was picked up by Mr. Phil Loeb one-- one day to go to the school, and he said to me, Mila, I have something-- I have something to show you. He made a mistake. He drove to the Everglades at that time. [LAUGHS] He was so excited about this letter that we made like a two-hour detour. We came two hours later. I had-- I had six sessions at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- I cried so in the car, and he gives me this-- this essay. And I would like you to-- this is the essay from the Steve Carns, that very boy who said he wants to be the captain of the Ku Klux Klan. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'How I Feel About the Holocaust-- Steve Carns. Before learning about the Holocaust, I felt extremely prejudiced against anyone that wasn't my race. I really never realized that people were actually tormented and murdered because of skin color or religious beliefs. I thought the cruelty of World War II was over and did not exist today. I hated blacks and wanted no part of any of them, much less the Jews. I listened and learned about what the Holocaust really meant, the worst type of-- of discrimination.

speaker: interviewer
text: While learning about it, I felt it was almost impossible to think that a nation could kill millions of innocent people for no reason. I was in disbelief that something so bad could happen. When we read about it from our text, it didn't make me feel like what happened was real. How could anything like that be real?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady survivor came to our class and discussed what happened to her in the concentration camps, and it made me feel like I was in the middle of it all. I felt so upset because the survivor had to relive the pain she went through to make us understand. Now that I am educated about the Holocaust, I no longer-- I no longer hate blacks because I know what being prejudiced and bigoted can do, which is only to destroy.' These are my words. These are my words that I told. And this is how I know that these kids are learning. 'I wanted to be head of the Ku Klux Klan, but not now. I want to stamp out racism. We all need to work together and help each other. I now-- I know now that here is still a lot of racism in our world, but I know I will not take part in it because I am now educated on the subject. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I even apologized to a black teacher for some ugly things I said to her earlier this year, and it made me feel good. I never want to be what I was before we studied and the Holocaust. I want to be a person who makes a difference in this world. This is what the study of the Holocaust has meant to me.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So it is written in the Torah, in our Bible, that 'to save one life is to save the world.' And I think that I got a lot of children out of a lot. I-- I have more letters, but I'm not going to-- I just would like to be my 'Death March' poem that I wrote. And [SIGHS] 'A herd of humans are driven through deserted streets. A spark of hope in our heart still beats. Where are we headed for? Where does our destination lie? We ask in muted voices, but hardly a sigh. We look at each other. Our eyes only speak. Are we going to make it, or have we reached our peak? We walk in hunger. We walk in pain, where cruelty and bloodshed know no restraint. Weeks go by. Our death march seems endless, driven by the Nazi murderers who reveled in the senseless destruction. The wounds in our bodies are covered with blood. The eyes of the dead are full of anger. Our trampled hearts cry out against our murderers. Will there be an end to our suffering and pain? Our eyes are searching the skies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in heaven a star lights up. I was fortunate to see that star. I was one of the girls in this death march-- Mila Bachner, survivor.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mila, I want to thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about my pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other final message that you would like to give before we stop to introduce your family and to show the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: My final message to the world [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is that we must find a way to conquer racism, hatred, bigotry, anti-Semitism. If we can accomplish that, then we have won the war. And if we don't, then I feel sorry for the world, for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mila. We're going to stop now and bring in your family. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As you see, this picture is still dirty from ashes. You see, it still is black. And we cannot clean this. This picture was given to me by my daughter's in-laws, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my daughter's husband's parents, by her cousin, a Mr. Tanenbaum, who was in Israel. And he went to visit the Warsaw after the war, and he found this picture in the ashes of the Warsaw ghetto. It was in such bad shape that I think half of it had to be taken off because it was falling apart, and I cannot touch it. So they cleaned it to the best of their ability, and I had it framed. It depicts Moses on the Nile how-- when he is being taken out by Pharaoh's sister, I believe. She took him out, and she raised him. It was made probably by-- by some beautiful Jewish mother in Warsaw, and I think it's the most beautiful thing that I have ever seen because it is so meaningful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is what this picture is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mrs. Horowitz, my-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] gave it to me because she said to me, Mila, I really don't have much use from it. But you, with speaking in schools, with being so involved, and you love these things, you'll appreciate it. I want you to have it as a gift. And I thanked her, and I took it. And I am very proud to have it hang in my living room.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture here is of my daddy, my mommy, and my dad's sister, my aunt [NON-ENGLISH]. It was taken at my cousin Rachel's wedding in Chrzanow, Poland in 1936. This is a picture of my daddy in 1940, when he worked slave labor work for the Germans in Chzarnow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's OK. No, no, leave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother Chaim, this boy. This [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is a friend of ours from home. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. This is one of my cousins [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from my-- my mother's nephew, this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this little boy here.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my brother Murray. This is my brother Murray, who I showed you the picture. This-- this little head over here. Who else do I know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know anybody else. Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chrzanow, in-- in-- in camp, in-- in the-- in a Jewish day camp. You see, it says there, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Jewish day camp,' you see? Right here. Do you know the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '30, 35. '35, '36. We went to camp when we were little bits [INAUDIBLE]. And I don't know the other people, but this is-- oh, you know something? These are the Roth girls. You see these twins here? This-- I cannot point it. I cannot see them here, even with the glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can point them much better there. You see these two little twins, one, two? We went to school together, but I don't think they are alive, none of them. Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roth-- I don't know their first names anymore. It's been such a long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But most of all, my little brother. He was such a good-looking kid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Such a sweet boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my sister, Mesia. She was 10 years old when she was taken to Auschwitz with her-- my daddy and her younger brother. This picture was taken for the IDs when the Nazis came into Poland. This is my brother, Moniek. His name now is Murray Zagorsky. He is the only one who survived this hell, and I found-- he found me after four years. He came to pick me up in-- in Bergen and took me to Austria, to the hospital where I recuperated. This is I in 1935, Mila Bachner. I saved these pictures on my neck during the Nazi reign of terror. A friend of mine brought it to [NON-ENGLISH] when we had-- judenrein and [NON-ENGLISH], which means that the-- the town was cleaned out from Jews. And this was taken when I needed it for my ID for the German camp card. This is the wedding. This is the wedding of my sister Malka. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] possibly-- it's September 1939, actually. And if you noticed here, you have the-- the Jewish stars and the white armband with the blue Star of David. That's one of my cousins, and this is the other one. This is Leib Goldstein and this is [NON-ENGLISH] Goldstein. This is my little brother, Nahman, this is her husband, Rolf Goldstein, and this is [NON-ENGLISH], And this is Mrs. Richter, who offered her garden for my sister to get married. This is little Jacob Richter. This is my cousin. This is my mommy. This is my daddy. This is my little sister. These are friends from Israel. They live there now. And these are the two girls, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], whose father came back from [NON-ENGLISH] to let us know that my brother died for stealing a piece of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they all perished--
